content.text: is a Python function wrapped in metadata (a name, a description, and an input schema) that an LLM can request by name. The LLM emits a
object; your agent executor invokes the matching function and returns a
with the result. This separation keeps the LLM stateless and lets you inject any external capability — web search, code execution, database queries — without changing the model.

attrs.alt: LangChain Tools and Toolkits architecture diagram
attrs.title: Agent calls a tool; the executor routes to the matching function and returns a ToolMessage

content.text: decorator is the simplest way to create a tool. It reads the function name (as the tool name), its docstring (as the description the LLM sees), and its type-annotated arguments (as the JSON schema) automatically.

from langchain_core.tools import tool

@tool
def get_word_length(word: str) -> int:
    """Return the number of characters in a word."""
    return len(word)

@tool
def add_numbers(a: float, b: float) -> float:
    """Add two numbers together and return the result."""
    return a + b

# Inspect the generated schema
print(get_word_length.name)          # 'get_word_length'
print(get_word_length.description)   # 'Return the number of characters in a word.'
print(get_word_length.args)          # {'word': {'title': 'Word', 'type': 'string'}}

# Call the tool directly (returns the raw value)
print(get_word_length.invoke("hello"))   # 5

# Tools are Runnables — they support invoke/ainvoke
import asyncio

@tool
async def async_fetch(url: str) -> str:
    """Fetch the content of a URL asynchronously."""
    import httpx
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        r = await client.get(url, timeout=10)
        return r.text[:500]
When a tool needs complex or deeply nested inputs, use
with an explicit Pydantic model. This gives full control over field descriptions, default values, and validators — all of which appear in the schema the LLM receives.

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from langchain_core.tools import StructuredTool
from typing import Literal

class SearchInput(BaseModel):
    query: str = Field(description="The search query string")
    num_results: int = Field(default=5, ge=1, le=20, description="Number of results to return")
    language: Literal["en", "fr", "de", "es"] = Field(
        default="en",
        description="ISO language code for the search locale",
    )

def web_search(query: str, num_results: int = 5, language: str = "en") -> list[dict]:
    """Search the web and return a list of results."""
    # In a real implementation, call a search API here
    return [{"title": f"Result {i} for '{query}'", "url": f"https://example.com/{i}"}
            for i in range(num_results)]

search_tool = StructuredTool.from_function(
    func=web_search,
    name="web_search",
    description="Search the web for up-to-date information on any topic.",
    args_schema=SearchInput,
)

# The LLM sees num_results and language as optional with defaults
print(search_tool.invoke({"query": "LangChain tools", "num_results": 3}))
Pass a list of tools to
. The LLM then knows about those tools and will emit
on its response when appropriate. Your code must check for these calls, execute them, and pass results back.

from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage, ToolMessage

llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001")
tools = [get_word_length, add_numbers, search_tool]
llm_with_tools = llm.bind_tools(tools)

# Build a tool-name → callable map for dispatch
tool_map = {t.name: t for t in tools}

def run_agent_turn(messages: list) -> list:
    """Run one turn: call LLM, execute any tool calls, return updated messages."""
    response = llm_with_tools.invoke(messages)
    messages = messages + [response]

    if not response.tool_calls:
        return messages   # no tools called — final answer

    # Execute each tool call and collect ToolMessages
    for call in response.tool_calls:
        tool_fn = tool_map[call["name"]]
        result  = tool_fn.invoke(call["args"])
        messages.append(
            ToolMessage(content=str(result), tool_call_id=call["id"])
        )

    return messages

# Multi-turn loop until no more tool calls
messages = [HumanMessage("How many letters are in the word 'refrigerator'?")]
while True:
    messages = run_agent_turn(messages)
    last = messages[-1]
    if hasattr(last, "tool_calls") and last.tool_calls:
        continue
    if not hasattr(last, "tool_calls"):
        break   # last message is a ToolMessage or AIMessage with no calls
    break

print(messages[-1].content)   # '12'
Tool Choice and Forcing a Tool
By default the LLM decides whether and which tool to call. You can override this with the

# Force the LLM to always call a tool (any tool)
llm_must_use_tool = llm.bind_tools(tools, tool_choice="any")

# Force a specific tool by name
llm_must_search = llm.bind_tools(tools, tool_choice="web_search")

# Let the LLM choose freely (default)
llm_auto = llm.bind_tools(tools, tool_choice="auto")

# Disable tools for this call
llm_no_tools = llm.bind_tools(tools, tool_choice="none")
package ships dozens of ready-to-use tools for common integrations:

# Wikipedia lookup (no API key needed)
from langchain_community.tools import WikipediaQueryRun
from langchain_community.utilities import WikipediaAPIWrapper

wiki = WikipediaQueryRun(api_wrapper=WikipediaAPIWrapper(top_k_results=2))
print(wiki.invoke("Alan Turing"))

# Tavily web search (requires TAVILY_API_KEY)
from langchain_community.tools.tavily_search import TavilySearchResults

tavily = TavilySearchResults(max_results=3)
results = tavily.invoke("LangChain 2024 updates")

# DuckDuckGo search (no key needed, rate-limited)
from langchain_community.tools import DuckDuckGoSearchRun
ddg = DuckDuckGoSearchRun()
print(ddg.invoke("latest AI news"))

# Python REPL (use with caution — executes arbitrary code)
from langchain_experimental.tools import PythonREPLTool
repl = PythonREPLTool()
print(repl.invoke("print(2 ** 10)"))   # 1024
is a class that owns a set of related tools and exposes them via
. Toolkits handle shared initialisation (connection strings, API clients) once so every tool can reuse the same session.

# SQL database toolkit — gives the agent read/write access to a DB
from langchain_community.agent_toolkits import SQLDatabaseToolkit
from langchain_community.utilities import SQLDatabase

db      = SQLDatabase.from_uri("sqlite:///my_database.db")
toolkit = SQLDatabaseToolkit(db=db, llm=llm)
tools   = toolkit.get_tools()
# tools → [QuerySQLDataBaseTool, InfoSQLDatabaseTool, ListSQLDatabaseTool, QuerySQLCheckerTool]

llm_with_db = llm.bind_tools(tools)

# File system toolkit — read/write/list files inside a sandboxed root
from langchain_community.agent_toolkits import FileManagementToolkit
import tempfile, os

with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as tmpdir:
    fs_toolkit = FileManagementToolkit(
        root_dir=tmpdir,
        selected_tools=["read_file", "write_file", "list_directory"],
    )
    fs_tools = fs_toolkit.get_tools()
    llm_with_fs = llm.bind_tools(fs_tools)
. The toolkit constructor receives the shared client or config; tools close over it.
If a tool raises an exception, the executor by default propagates it and crashes the agent loop. Set
to catch exceptions and return them as a string to the LLM instead — letting the agent decide how to recover.
directly. No LLM, no network, no API key required in unit tests.
Tool Calling in an Agent Loop with LangGraph
In practice, tools are wired into a LangGraph agent using the built-in
from the LLM node, executes every
objects for the next LLM turn.

from typing import Annotated
import operator
from langchain_core.messages import BaseMessage, HumanMessage
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from langgraph.prebuilt import ToolNode

# Define the graph state as a message list
class AgentState(dict):
    messages: Annotated[list[BaseMessage], operator.add]

# Nodes
def call_llm(state: AgentState) -> AgentState:
    response = llm_with_tools.invoke(state["messages"])
    return {"messages": [response]}

tool_node = ToolNode(tools)   # handles dispatch + ToolMessage creation automatically

def should_continue(state: AgentState) -> str:
    last_message = state["messages"][-1]
    if last_message.tool_calls:
        return "tools"
    return END

# Build graph
graph = StateGraph(AgentState)
graph.add_node("llm", call_llm)
graph.add_node("tools", tool_node)
graph.set_entry_point("llm")
graph.add_conditional_edges("llm", should_continue, {"tools": "tools", END: END})
graph.add_edge("tools", "llm")   # loop back after tool execution

agent = graph.compile()

# Run
result = agent.invoke({"messages": [HumanMessage("What's 48 characters in 'supercalifragilistic'?")]})
print(result["messages"][-1].content)
also handles parallel tool calls: if the LLM emits multiple
runs them concurrently and returns one
wraps a Python function with a name, description, and JSON schema so an LLM can call it by name.
reads the signature and docstring automatically;
injects the schemas into the LLM's context; the executor dispatches
Toolkits group related tools that share an initialised client; implement
to return exceptions as strings to the LLM instead of crashing the agent loop.
— no LLM or API key needed.
